visits.text: Drake Bay (Bahía Drake) is accessible only by boat from Sierpe (~1h) or by small plane from San José. Your car stays behind in Sierpe at a guarded parking lot. Drake Bay village serves as the launching point for exploring the Osa Peninsula—one of the most biodiverse spots on the planet. Plan minimum 2 nights.

Corcovado National Park, described by National Geographic as the most biologically intense place on Earth, spans 41,788 hectares of primary rainforest. It contains 2.5% of the world's biodiversity on 0.001% of Earth's surface. Jaguars, Baird's tapirs, pumas, scarlet macaws, and four monkey species coexist in this lowland forest.

Isla del Caño, a biological reserve 20 km offshore from Drake Bay, is one of Costa Rica's premier diving and snorkeling sites. Whitetip reef sharks, sea turtles, manta rays, dolphins. The pre-Columbian stone spheres scattered across the island remain an archaeological enigma.

activities.text: First evening in Drake Bay. Sunset walk along the main beach. Lodges offer guided tours for the following day—Corcovado or Isla del Caño, book upon arrival.

Corcovado or Isla del Caño—both are mutually exclusive for one day. Corcovado for hiking enthusiasts and terrestrial wildlife (jaguars, tapirs, scarlet macaws). Isla del Caño for divers and marine life lovers.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Costa Rica: Caribbean, Volcanoes, Cloud Forests & Pacific Coast
Complete Costa Rica: Caribbean, Volcanoes, Cloud Forests & Pacific Coast

visits.text: The San José–La Fortuna drive cuts through the Central Valley before hugging Lake Arenal's shore in its final stretch. Arenal's perfect cone rises ahead: 1,670 meters of grey basalt commanding the plain.
Downtown La Fortuna is compact: a white church, a park, and a row of tour agencies. It's the ideal launching pad for three days exploring the region.

The Arenal 1968 trail bears the name of the July eruption that buried three villages under molten rock. Two routes to choose from: Sendero Bosque (4.7 km through the forest) or Sendero Colada (4 km, straight toward the lava fields). The fractured black terrain contrasts sharply with the vegetation reclaiming its territory.
Mirador El Silencio, accessible from the 1968 trail, delivers one of the most direct angles on Arenal's cone. On clear days, you'll also spot Lake Arenal stretching below.

Mistico Hanging Bridges Park weaves 3 km of trails across 16 bridges—six of them suspended above the canopy. Guides armed with spotting scopes pick out sloths, toucans, and howler monkeys in the treetops.
La Fortuna Waterfall plunges 70 meters into an emerald pool. From the paid entrance, it's roughly 500 steps down to reach it, then the same climb back up—a genuine workout in the heat.

Cerro Chato is Arenal's dormant neighbor, accessed from the Observatory Lodge. Its crater holds a blue-tinted lake where swimming is possible. The hike is demanding and inadvisable in rainy conditions.

La Fortuna sits at the base of Volcán Arenal as the staging ground for exploring the volcano zone. Arenal (1,657 m) erupted continuously from 1968 to 2010—it's now in a resting phase but remains active. Solidified lava flows are visible from the Arenal 1968 trail.

La Fortuna Waterfall, fed by runoff from the volcano's northern slopes, plunges 70 meters into a turquoise pool reached by descending 500-530 steps. Going down takes 15-20 minutes; coming back up demands proper footwear and functioning lungs.

The Arenal 1968 trail (4 to 4.7 km, 1h30 to 3h) crosses the solidified lava flows from the founding eruption. Two route options: Sendero Bosque (regenerating forest) and Sendero Colada (open lava field). Direct views of the volcano's cone when skies cooperate.

The Mistico Hanging Bridges (3 km, 16 bridges including 6 suspended) traverse the rainforest canopy. Walking height ranges from 40 to 80 meters above ground—prime territory for spotting birds and monkeys from the treetops.

The drive from San José to La Fortuna cuts through the Central Valley before hugging Lake Arenal's shoreline. Arenal's textbook cone emerges on approach: 1,670 meters of grey basalt commanding the plain below.
Downtown La Fortuna is compact—a white-steepled church, a leafy plaza, and a lineup of tour operators. It's the perfect base camp for three days of volcano country exploration.

The Arenal 1968 Trail takes its name from the July eruption that buried three villages under molten rock. Two routes to choose from: Sendero Bosque (4.7 km through the forest) or Sendero Colada (4 km, straight toward the lava fields). Black fractured terrain gives way to forest reclaiming its territory.
Mirador El Silencio, accessed from the 1968 Trail, offers one of the most direct angles on Arenal's cone. On clear days, Lake Arenal spreads out below.

Mistico Hanging Bridges features 3 km of trails punctuated by 16 bridges—six of them suspended high above the canopy. Guides armed with spotting scopes pick out sloths, toucans, and howler monkeys in the branches.
La Fortuna Waterfall plunges 70 meters into an emerald pool. The entrance fee gets you down some 500 steps to the base—and back up again. The climb is real in the midday heat.

Cerro Chato is Arenal's dormant neighbor, accessed via the Observatory Lodge trailhead. Its crater cradles a blue lake where swimming is possible. The hike is demanding and should be avoided in rainy conditions.

activities.text: First evening in La Fortuna: stroll around the central park, dinner at one of the local restaurants with views of the volcano.
Settle in early—tomorrow kicks off with the Arenal 1968 hike at dawn, before clouds swallow the summit.

Late afternoon, head to the natural hot springs on the volcano's flanks. The water, heated by geothermal activity, ranges from 25 to 38°C. A free thermal river flows near Tabacon Hot Springs, accessible from the roadside.
Bogarin Trail is a private path on La Fortuna's edge, renowned for two-toed and three-toed sloth sightings. Local guides, equipped with telescopes, point out animals camouflaged in the branches.
Lake Arenal, created in 1972, is Costa Rica's largest. Its north shore offers water-level views of the volcano. Local outfitters provide kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, and boat tours.

Evening at the natural hot springs heated by volcanic activity. Multiple options at different price points: the public Tabacón springs are the most famous, while the free springs along Rio Chollin downstream can be accessed independently.

Kayaking or windsurfing on Lake Arenal. The artificial lake (85 km²) is framed by the volcano to the east and green hills to the west—prevailing winds make it one of Central America's premier windsurfing destinations.
First evening in La Fortuna: stroll the plaza, then settle in for dinner at one of the local restaurants with volcano views framed by the windows.
Get to bed early—tomorrow starts with the Arenal 1968 Trail at dawn, before clouds roll in and swallow the summit.

Late in the day, head to the natural hot springs at the volcano's base. Geothermal activity heats the water to between 25 and 38°C. A free thermal river flows near the Tabacon Hot Springs resort—accessible from the roadside.
The Bogarin Trail is a private path on the edge of La Fortuna, known for sightings of two- and three-toed sloths. Local guides equipped with telescopes point out animals camouflaged in the branches.
Lake Arenal, created in 1972, is Costa Rica's largest. The northern shore offers volcano views from the water. Local outfitters run kayak, stand-up paddle, and boat tours.

Costa Rica Road Trip: Volcanoes, Cloud Forests & Pacific Beaches

visits.text: Manuel Antonio National Park is Costa Rica's smallest (683 hectares of land) but one of its richest in biodiversity. The main trail, flat and well-maintained, follows an 18-hectare mangrove before reaching Playa Manuel Antonio, considered among the country's finest beaches.

Punta Catedral is a former island connected to the mainland by a sand tombolo, forming a whale-tail-shaped peninsula. The trail circling it offers views of both beaches and the park's 12 islands, seabird sanctuaries.
Biesanz Bay, a small protected cove minutes from the hillside hotels, is ideal for relaxed snorkeling: sandy bottom, clear water, rays and parrotfish frequently spotted.

The descent from Monteverde to the Central Pacific crosses Guanacaste's agricultural plains before meeting the coast. Heat replaces cloud-forest humidity. Quepos, a small port 5 km from the park, serves as the region's base town.

Manuel Antonio National Park is Costa Rica's smallest (683 terrestrial hectares) but one of its richest. The main trail, flat and well-maintained, skirts an 18-hectare mangrove before reaching Playa Manuel Antonio, considered among the country's finest beaches.

Punta Catedral is a former island linked to the mainland by a sand tombolo, forming a whale-tail peninsula. The loop trail offers views of both beaches and the park's 12 islands, which serve as seabird sanctuaries.
The park's mangrove, visible from trails paralleling the beaches, covers 18 hectares of fresh and salt water. Red and white mangroves form a distinct ecosystem, separate from the adjacent rainforest.

Biesanz Bay, a few minutes' walk from the hillside hotels, is a small cove protected from the waves. The water is clear, the bottom sandy—ideal for snorkeling. Rays, parrotfish, and occasionally sea turtles are spotted.

Manuel Antonio National Park is Costa Rica's smallest (683 terrestrial hectares) but one of its biodiversity heavyweights. The main trail skirts an 18-hectare mangrove before reaching Playa Manuel Antonio, widely considered among the country's most stunning beaches.

Punta Catedral is a former island now tethered to the mainland by a sand tombolo forming a peninsula. The trail circling it offers views of both flanking beaches and the park's 12 offshore islands—sanctuaries for seabirds.
Biesanz Bay, a protected cove minutes from the hillside hotels, is ideal for relaxed snorkeling: sandy bottom, clear water, rays and parrotfish making regular appearances.

The descent from Monteverde toward the Central Pacific cuts across the Guanacaste agricultural plain before meeting the coast. Cloud-forest humidity gives way to coastal heat. Quepos, a small port town 5 km from the park, serves as the area's base.

Manuel Antonio National Park is Costa Rica's smallest (683 hectares on land) yet one of its richest. The main trail, flat and well-maintained, follows an 18-hectare mangrove before reaching Playa Manuel Antonio—widely considered one of the country's finest beaches.

Punta Catedral is a former island connected to the mainland by a sand tombolo, forming a whale-tail peninsula. The loop trail offers views of both beaches and the park's 12 offshore islands, which serve as seabird sanctuaries.
The park's mangrove, visible from trails paralleling the beaches, covers 18 hectares of fresh and saltwater. Red and white mangroves form their own ecosystem, distinct from the adjacent rainforest.

Biesanz Bay, a few minutes' walk from the hillside hotels, is a small cove protected from the surf. The water is clear, the bottom sandy—ideal for snorkeling. Rays, parrotfish, and sometimes sea turtles make appearances.
Playa Gemelas, a small cove inside the park, is Manuel Antonio's least-visited beach. Perfect for a quiet morning between forest and Pacific, after the big hiking days.

activities.text: Hire a certified naturalist guide at the park entrance. Equipped with a spotting scope, they'll locate two-toed sloths, white-faced capuchin monkeys, and the squirrel monkeys endemic to Manuel Antonio—virtually impossible to find on your own in the dense vegetation.
Swimming at Playa Manuel Antonio after the hike. Snorkeling possible on the sides of the beach to spot moray eels, angelfish, and starfish.
Kayak excursion through the Isla Damas mangrove. The channels of the Río Damas estuary harbor crocodiles, monkeys, egrets, and terns. Local operators provide equipment and guides.
Free afternoon in Manuel Antonio. The Quepos marina offers offshore sport fishing, coastal tours, and sea kayak rentals.
Evening, head to Playa Espadilla Norte, the public beach outside the park. It's the classic spot to watch the Pacific sunset. Palms, gentle waves, raking light—Costa Rica's postcard image.
Quepos comes alive at night. The town is known for its fresh-catch restaurants and relaxed atmosphere. Stroll the waterfront before dinner.

Hire a certified naturalist guide at the park entrance. Armed with a spotting scope, they'll locate two-toed sloths clinging to branches, white-headed capuchin monkeys, and squirrel monkeys—endemic to Manuel Antonio. The difference between a solo visit and a guided one is considerable.
After the hike, swim at Playa Manuel Antonio. The turquoise waters are sheltered by overhanging forest. Snorkeling is possible on the beach's flanks to observe moray eels, angelfish, and sea stars.

The Isla Damas mangrove kayak tour descends the Río Damas estuary over half a day. The network of channels shelters crocodiles, monkeys, shorebirds, and terns. Local outfitters provide equipment and guides.
Free afternoon at the beach. Quepos has an active marina: offshore sportfishing, sea kayak rentals, and coastal tours launch from there.

Certified naturalist guide right at park entrance: armed with a spotting scope, they locate two-toed sloths, white-headed capuchin monkeys, and the endemic Central American squirrel monkey found only in Manuel Antonio.
Swimming at Playa Manuel Antonio. Snorkeling possible on the beach's flanks for moray eels, angelfish, and starfish.
Kayak excursion through the Isla Damas mangrove. The channels threading through the Río Damas estuary shelter crocodiles, monkeys, egrets, and terns. Early departure for peak wildlife activity.
Afternoon at leisure. Quepos marina offers sport fishing, coastal tours, and sea kayak rentals. Sunset at Playa Espadilla Norte.

Come evening, head to Playa Espadilla Norte, the public beach outside the park boundaries. This is the classic spot to watch the sun drop into the Pacific. Palms, gentle surf, golden light—Costa Rica's postcard image made real.
Quepos comes alive at night. The town is known for fresh-catch restaurants and a relaxed vibe. Stroll the waterfront before dinner.

Hire a certified naturalist guide at the park entrance. Armed with a spotting scope, they'll pick out two-toed sloths clinging to branches, white-headed capuchin monkeys, and Central American squirrel monkeys—endemic to Manuel Antonio. The difference between a solo visit and a guided one is night and day.
After the hike, swim at Playa Manuel Antonio. The turquoise water is sheltered by overhanging forest. Snorkeling is possible at the beach's edges to spot morays, angelfish, and starfish.

The kayaking excursion through Isla Damas mangrove follows the Río Damas estuary for half a day. The network of channels shelters crocodiles, monkeys, shorebirds, and terns. Local outfitters provide gear and guides.
Free afternoon at the beach. Quepos has an active marina: deep-sea sportfishing, sea-kayak rentals, and coastal tours depart from here.

Board a catamaran for a cruise along the Pacific coast. The route follows the national park islands, with a snorkeling stop in waters rich with marine life. Coastal dolphins regularly accompany the boats; in season (July–November), humpback whales pass through the area.
Final evening in Quepos: dinner on the waterfront with catch-of-the-day. The town is lively at night, with several restaurants along the water.

visits.text: The La Fortuna–Monteverde road traces Lake Arenal's northern rim. It's one of the country's most scenic drives: the volcano shrinking in your rearview, the Tilarán winds picking up, the road climbing steadily toward cloud forest.
Santa Elena is Monteverde's base village. A few commercial streets gather restaurants, tour agencies, and lodgings. The vibe is more laid-back than La Fortuna—a mountain town at 1,400 meters.

Santa Elena Reserve, less trafficked than its Monteverde neighbor, spans 310 hectares at 1,600 meters. On clear days, its lookout delivers views of Arenal volcano and, weather permitting, both coasts.

Monteverde Cloud Forest Biological Reserve spreads across 10,500 hectares at 1,400 meters. Ancient trees vanish into mist, orchids—over 500 species—colonize their trunks. Nine marked trails thread through this singular ecosystem, including the Sendero Bosque Nuboso, ideal for spotting the resplendent quetzal.

Curi-Cancha Reserve, 3 km from Santa Elena, sees fewer visitors than Monteverde but matches its richness. Seven marked trails cross 76 hectares of primary forest. Spider monkeys, capuchins, coatis, and quetzals are regularly observed.
Monteverde Butterfly Garden showcases around 490 species in a netted enclosure. Chrysalises mid-emergence are particularly dramatic. Both kids and adults enjoy the life-cycle demonstrations.

The Monteverde Cloud Forest Biological Reserve (10,500 hectares, elevation ~1,400 m) ranks among the world's most celebrated cloud forests. Perched atop the Tilarán Range, it shelters more than 400 bird species including the Resplendent Quetzal, 100 mammal species, and 500 orchid varieties.

The Curi-Cancha Reserve (76 hectares), less trampled than Monteverde, delivers similar wildlife encounters in a quieter setting. The cloud forest is equally dense, with excellent sightings of hummingbirds, howler monkeys, and sloths.

The La Fortuna–Monteverde road follows Lake Arenal's northern shore. It's one of the country's most scenic drives: the volcano in your rearview, the Tilarán winds picking up, the road climbing steadily toward cloud forest.

Santa Elena is Monteverde's base village. A handful of commercial streets gather restaurants, tour agencies, and lodging. The vibe is more laid-back than La Fortuna—a mountain town sitting at 1,400 meters.

Santa Elena Reserve sees fewer visitors than its Monteverde neighbor yet sprawls across 310 hectares at 1,600 meters. On clear days, its mirador delivers views of Arenal and—conditions permitting—both coasts.

Monteverde Cloud Forest Reserve stretches across more than 10,500 hectares at 1,400 meters. Centuries-old trees vanish into mist, orchids—over 500 species—cling to their trunks. Nine marked trails thread through this unique ecosystem, including Sendero Bosque Nuboso, your best shot at spotting the resplendent quetzal.

Curi-Cancha Reserve, 3 km from Santa Elena, draws smaller crowds than Monteverde and rivals it for biodiversity. Seven marked trails wind through 76 hectares of primary forest. Spider monkeys, capuchins, coatis, and quetzals appear regularly.
Monteverde Butterfly Garden showcases around 490 species in screened enclosures. Chrysalises mid-emergence are particularly spectacular. Children and adults alike enjoy the life-cycle demonstrations.

activities.text: Evening in Santa Elena: wander the village, sample the local cheese—a specialty of the Quaker families who settled here in the 1950s. Artisan dairies sell directly to the public.

Monteverde's canopy tour ranks among Costa Rica's most celebrated. The ziplines traverse the canopy over several kilometers; some cables exceed 700 meters. The circuit also includes suspension bridges and, for the bold, a Tarzan swing.
Early morning guided hike in search of the Resplendent Quetzal—local guides spot them where solo visitors walk right past. The morning mist wraps the forest in an otherworldly atmosphere.
Canopy tour and ziplines above the cloud forest. Monteverde birthed the canopy tour in Costa Rica—several operators run circuits of 10 to 20 cables with views across the Gulf of Nicoya on clear days.

Monteverde's canopy tour ranks among Costa Rica's most celebrated. Ziplines cross the canopy for several kilometers; some cables stretch beyond 700 meters. The circuit also includes suspension bridges and, for the bold, a Tarzan swing.

visits.text: The return from Tortuguero involves a boat ride back to La Pavona, then a drive toward the Caribbean coast past Limón. Puerto Viejo de Talamanca is about 5-6 hours total travel time. The road hugs the coast after Limón—your first glimpse of Costa Rica's Atlantic side, very different from the Pacific: greener water, jungle straight down to the sand.

Puerto Viejo is a former fishing village that's become the epicenter of the southern Caribbean coast. Its Afro-Caribbean, Bribri, and Tico blend creates an atmosphere you won't find anywhere else in Costa Rica—reggae music, vivid colors, rondón (Caribbean coconut milk soup) served in roadside shacks.

Cahuita National Park, 20 km from Puerto Viejo, protects one of Costa Rica's best-preserved coral reefs. The reef surrounds Punta Cahuita: 35 coral species including some colonies reaching 2 meters in diameter, 123 fish species, nurse sharks, and manta rays. A 1991 earthquake lifted the coast by one meter, damaging part of the coral—but the reef is regenerating.

The park's coastal trail (8 km from Kelly Creek to Puerto Vargas) follows the beach, alternating between humid tropical forest and oceanfront. Sloths, howler monkeys, raccoons, iguanas, and countless bird species are regularly spotted. The park is community-managed and operates on a voluntary donation basis—entry is free.

The road south of Puerto Viejo passes a succession of beaches for 13 km to Manzanillo. Punta Uva is the most stunning in the area: turquoise water, golden sand, jungle right down to the shore. Playa Manzanillo, within the Refugio Nacional Gandoca-Manzanillo, marks the southern boundary of the protected zone.

The Gandoca-Manzanillo Refuge protects 10 km of beach, 740 hectares of forest, a coral reef, and two marshes. It's also a nesting area for sea turtles, manatees, crocodiles, and dolphins. Marked trails run through the forest from Manzanillo village.

The return from Tortuguero goes by boat to La Pavona, then by car toward the Caribbean coast along Limón. Puerto Viejo de Talamanca is about 5–6 hours total travel time. The road hugs the coast past Limón: a first glimpse of Costa Rica's Atlantic side, very different from the Pacific—greener sea, jungle reaching the sand.

Puerto Viejo is a former fishing village turned epicenter of the southern Caribbean coast. Its Afro-Caribbean, Bribri, and Tico blend creates an atmosphere found nowhere else in Costa Rica—reggae music, vivid colors, rondón (Caribbean coconut-milk soup) in little roadside eateries.

Cahuita National Park, 20 km from Puerto Viejo, protects one of Costa Rica's best-preserved coral reefs. The reef encircles Punta Cahuita: 35 coral species—some reaching 2 meters in diameter—123 fish species, nurse sharks, and manta rays. A 1991 earthquake lifted the coast one meter, damaging part of the corals—but the reef is recovering.

The park's coastal trail (8 km from Kelly Creek to Puerto Vargas) hugs the beach, alternating between rainforest and shoreline. Sloths, howler monkeys, raccoons, iguanas, and countless birds are observed. The park is community-managed and operates on voluntary donation—free entrance.

The road south of Puerto Viejo runs along a succession of beaches for 13 km to Manzanillo. Punta Uva is the area's finest: turquoise water, golden sand, jungle reaching the shore. Playa Manzanillo, within Refugio Nacional Gandoca-Manzanillo, marks the protected zone's southern boundary.

Refugio Gandoca-Manzanillo protects 10 km of beach, 740 hectares of forest, a coral reef, and two wetlands. It's also a nesting zone for sea turtles, manatees, crocodiles, and dolphins. Marked trails cross the forest from Manzanillo village.

Leaving Tortuguero reverses your arrival: boat back to La Pavona, then drive south along the Caribbean coast past Limón (~5h30 total). Puerto Viejo de Talamanca began as a fishing village and evolved into the beating heart of Costa Rica's southern Caribbean—Afro-Caribbean soul, reggae rhythms drifting from beach bars, coconut milk in everything worth eating.

Cahuita National Park protects one of Costa Rica's healthiest coral reefs: 35 coral species, 123 fish species, nurse sharks cruising the shallows, and manta rays gliding past on invisible currents. The coastal trail (8 km from Kelly Creek to Puerto Vargas) alternates between beach walking and rainforest shade. Sloths, howler monkeys, raccoons, and iguanas make regular cameos.

The road south of Puerto Viejo traces a necklace of beaches for 13 km to Manzanillo. Punta Uva stands out as the jewel: turquoise water lapping golden sand, jungle pressing right to the tideline. The Gandoca-Manzanillo Wildlife Refuge safeguards 10 km of coastline, 740 hectares of forest, a coral reef, two wetlands, and nesting grounds for sea turtles, manatees, and dolphins.

activities.text: First evening in Puerto Viejo: explore the waterfront on foot or by bike. Bike rental is the local mode of transport—distances between beaches are short and the road is flat.
Snorkeling trip with a certified guide on the Cahuita reef, accessible only by boat with an escort. Local guides offer 2-hour sessions from the beach with all equipment provided.
Return to Puerto Viejo for a free afternoon at Playa Cocles, the area's most popular surf beach. Waves suitable for all levels—locals offer year-round lessons.
Full-day bike ride along the Puerto Viejo-Manzanillo coastal road. The 13 km stretch is flat, shaded, and punctuated with beaches. It's the best way to explore the coast at the local pace.
Snorkeling at Punta Uva or Manzanillo. These are the two best snorkeling spots on the Caribbean coast after Cahuita: coral reefs, tropical fish, rays, and sometimes sea turtles.
First evening in Puerto Viejo: explore the waterfront on foot or by bike. Bike rental is the quintessential local transport—distances between beaches are short and the road is flat.
Snorkeling outing with a certified guide on Cahuita reef, accessible only by boat with an escort. Local guides offer 2-hour sessions from the beach with all equipment provided.
Return to Puerto Viejo for a free late afternoon at Playa Cocles, the area's most renowned surf beach. Waves suit all levels—locals offer lessons year-round.
Full-day bike ride along the Puerto Viejo–Manzanillo coastal road. The 13 km are flat, shaded, and dotted with beaches. It's the best way to explore the coast at the local pace.
Snorkeling at Punta Uva or Manzanillo. These are the Caribbean coast's two best mask-and-snorkel spots after Cahuita: coral reefs, tropical fish, rays, and sometimes sea turtles.
First evening in Puerto Viejo: explore the oceanfront on foot or by bicycle. Bike rental is the local currency here—distances between beaches are short and the road runs flat as a paddleboard.
Snorkeling excursion with a certified guide to Cahuita's reef, accessible only by boat. Two-hour sessions with all equipment provided.
Return to Puerto Viejo for late afternoon at Playa Cocles, a renowned surf beach where the Salsa Brava wave breaks over shallow reef—a magnet for experienced surfers from December through April.

Bike day from Puerto Viejo to Manzanillo (13 km flat and shaded) with beach-hopping stops. Snorkeling at Punta Uva or Manzanillo—among the best mask-and-fin spots on the Caribbean coast after Cahuita.

visits.text: San José is the pivot point between the two coasts. The Puerto Viejo-San José drive (approx. 4h30) crosses Braulio Carrillo Park, recognizable by its hyper-dense vegetation and the Zurquí Tunnel—one of the few passages through the central mountain range.

San José's National Theater, inaugurated in 1897, was financed by a coffee tax imposed on Central Valley producers. Its neoclassical facade opens onto an Italian-style hall decorated with imported marble and paintings commissioned from European artists.

Beneath Plaza de la Cultura, the Pre-Columbian Gold Museum houses more than 1,600 pieces created by the peoples who lived in Costa Rica before the Spanish arrival: animal figurines, ritual jewelry, fine gold pendants. The underground architecture of the museum is itself remarkable.
The Jade Museum, facing Plaza de la Democracia, holds the largest collection of pre-Columbian jade in the Americas. The jadeite pendants, masks, and figurines date from 500 BC to 800 AD.

San José serves as the pivot between the Pacific and the Caribbean. The Manuel Antonio–San José route (~2.5–3 hours) allows for a late-morning arrival, a free afternoon in the city, and an early departure to La Pavona the next day to catch the first boat to Tortuguero (departure 7:30am).

San José is the pivot between the two coasts. The Puerto Viejo–San José route (approx. 4.5 hours) crosses Braulio Carrillo Park, recognizable by its hyper-dense vegetation and the Zurquí tunnel—one of the few passages through the Central Cordillera.
San José serves as the pivot point between Caribbean coast and volcanic highlands. The Puerto Viejo–San José route (~4h30) punches through Braulio Carrillo National Park via the Zurquí tunnel.

The National Theatre (1897) stands as a monument to Costa Rica's coffee-fueled golden age: neoclassical facade, Italian-style auditorium, imported marble, and paintings commissioned from European artists when coffee money flowed like rivers.

Pivot stop in San José between the Highlands and Pacific coast. The Jade Museum, facing Plaza de la Democracia, houses the Americas' largest collection of pre-Columbian jade: jadeite figurines, pendants, and ritual masks dating from 500 BC to 800 AD.

The Pre-Columbian Gold Museum, beneath Plaza de la Cultura, displays over 1,600 fine gold pieces created by Costa Rica's pre-Columbian peoples: animal figurines, ritual jewelry, pendants crafted between 700 and 1500 AD.

Return to San José (~3h30 from Uvita via the Inter-American Highway, which crests Cerro de la Muerte at 3,400 m elevation—the national route's highest point, often shrouded in clouds). The descent into San José delivers dramatic views across the Central Valley.
Final morning before your flight. The Pre-Columbian Gold Museum beneath Plaza de la Cultura and the National Theatre are both within walking distance of downtown hotels.

The National Theatre of San José, inaugurated in 1897, was financed by a coffee tax imposed on Central Valley growers. Its neoclassical facade opens onto an Italian-style auditorium adorned with imported marble and paintings commissioned from European artists. Visit by day or catch an evening performance.

Beneath the Plaza de la Cultura, the Pre-Columbian Gold Museum gathers more than 1,600 pieces—animal figurines, ritual jewelry, frog-shaped pendants—created by the peoples who lived in Costa Rica before the Spanish arrived. The museum's subterranean architecture is remarkable in its own right.

The Jade Museum, facing Plaza de la Democracia, houses the Americas' largest collection of pre-Columbian jade. Jadeite pendants, masks, and figurines date from 500 BC to AD 800. The five-story contemporary building also includes ceramics and stone objects in their ritual contexts.

The Mercado Central, steps from the National Theatre, has been the city's covered market since 1880. Tropical fruit, herbs, pottery, and daily casado sit side by side with vendors of roasted coffee. The atmosphere is everyday tico life, far removed from tourism.

Barrio Amón, north of downtown, lines up Victorian and Art Deco mansions from the early 20th century. A few galleries and cafés occupy the old homes of coffee-baron families. A fine end-of-trip stroll.

activities.text: Arrive late morning. Free afternoon in the center: Barrio Amón for its Victorian houses, Mercado Central for lunch, Plaza de la Cultura for a first urban taste before descending to the Pacific.

Final morning in San José before your flight. Stroll along Avenida Central between Plaza de la Cultura and Parque Central. Stop at Mercado Central for a last Costa Rican coffee and end-of-trip shopping.

Free afternoon in San José: Barrio Amón, Mercado Central, or the Jade Museum depending on your interests. Prep your bags for Tortuguero—bring cash (no ATMs on-site) and book the La Pavona boat if not already arranged.
Final morning in San José returning from the Caribbean. Stroll along Avenida Central, stop at Mercado Central for a last Costa Rican coffee and end-of-trip shopping before the airport transfer.
Arrive late morning. Lunch at Mercado Central, tour the Teatro Nacional early afternoon, then drive toward La Fortuna (~3h).
Arrive mid-morning from Monteverde (~3h30). Afternoon museums or stroll through Barrio Amón before descending to Manuel Antonio.
Final evening in San José. Dinner in Barrio Escalante, the capital's gastronomic quarter, or at Mercado Central to stay rooted in local authenticity. Pack for tomorrow's flight.
Final stroll along Avenida Central. Stop at Mercado Central for a last Costa Rican coffee and end-of-trip shopping before transferring to Juan Santamaría Airport.

Wander Avenida Central, the pedestrian street connecting Plaza de la Cultura to Parque Central. Colorful buildings, live music, and sugarcane juice vendors compose the scene of a final Costa Rican afternoon.
Parque Central is downtown San José's green lung. Families of green parrots nest in the trees and appear each afternoon—a distinctly tico urban quirk.

visits.text: The journey from San José to Tortuguero unfolds in two stages: a roughly 3-hour drive to the La Pavona boat dock, then a 90-minute river cruise along the canals of the Rio La Suerte and Tortuguero River. Both boat segments are experiences in themselves—howler monkeys swinging through the treetops, herons stalking the muddy banks, vegetation closing overhead like a living tunnel.

Tortuguero is a village of roughly 1,800 people sandwiched on a narrow strip of land between the Caribbean Sea and the national park canals. No cars allowed—people get around on foot, by bicycle, or by boat. The main walkway hosts a handful of restaurants, souvenir shops, and tour operators.

Tortuguero National Park spans 76,937 hectares, more than 50,000 of which are marine. Its network of canals snakes through humid tropical rainforest often called Costa Rica's mini-Amazon. The waterways—Caño Chiquero (2.3 km), Caño Harold, Caño Palma (5.1 km)—can be navigated by canoe or kayak without motors to preserve the silence.

The El Jaguar land trail (2.5 km, running parallel to the beach) cuts through humid tropical forest from the village. Waterproof boots are available to rent at the entrance—essential during wet conditions.

Cerro Tortuguero is a small 120-meter hill accessible by boat then on foot from the village. Its summit offers the only panoramic view in the region: Caribbean Sea, network of canals, and endless forest.

The San José–Tortuguero journey happens in two stages: road to La Pavona dock (~3 hours), then boat along the Río La Suerte and Tortuguero River canals (~1.5 hours). The two boat rides through the jungle are visits in themselves: howler monkeys in the trees, herons on the banks, vegetation closing overhead.

Tortuguero is a village of roughly 1,800 people squeezed onto a sliver of land between the Caribbean Sea and the park's canals. No cars: you get around on foot, by bike, or by boat. The main street gathers a few restaurants, souvenir shops, and tour operators.

Tortuguero National Park covers 76,937 hectares, over 50,000 at sea. Its canal network snakes through a rainforest nicknamed Costa Rica's little Amazon. The water trails—Caño Chiquero (2.3 km), Caño Harold, Caño Palma (5.1 km)—are navigable by canoe or kayak without motors to preserve silence.
The El Jaguar land trail (2.5 km, parallel to the beach) threads through the rainforest from the village. Waterproof boots are available for rent at the entrance—essential in wet conditions.

Cerro Tortuguero is a small 120-meter hill accessible by boat then on foot from the village. Its summit offers the region's only panoramic view: Caribbean Sea, canal network, and forest stretching to the horizon.

The journey from San José to Tortuguero unfolds in two stages: drive to the La Pavona dock (~3h), then glide by boat through the Rio La Suerte and Tortuguero River canals (~1h30). Both boat segments feel less like transportation and more like your first wildlife safari—howler monkeys roar from overhead branches, herons stalk the muddy banks, and the jungle canopy closes in above the water like a green cathedral.

Tortuguero village sits on a narrow strip of land pinched between the Caribbean Sea and the national park canals—home to roughly 1,800 people and zero cars. You navigate by foot, bicycle, or boat. The main walkway runs through the heart of town, lined with Caribbean restaurants, souvenir shops, and tour operators offering everything from night turtle walks to dawn kayak expeditions.

Tortuguero National Park sprawls across 76,937 hectares—more than 50,000 of them submerged. Its labyrinth of waterways snakes through rainforest so dense it's been dubbed Costa Rica's Little Amazon. The aquatic trails—Caño Chiquero (2.3 km), Caño Harold, Caño Palma (5.1 km)—are navigable by canoe or motorless kayak, where silence is your passport to wildlife encounters.

The El Jaguar trail (2.5 km) cuts through primary rainforest from the village edge. Waterproof rubber boots are available for rent at the entrance—essential when the trail turns to boot-sucking mud after rain.

Cerro Tortuguero rises 120 meters as the only viewpoint in an otherwise pancake-flat region. Accessible by boat then on foot from the village, the summit delivers the region's sole panoramic vista: Caribbean Sea stretching east, the canal network threading west, and unbroken jungle canopy rolling to the horizon.

activities.text: Arrive mid-afternoon. First evening in the village: stroll the main walkway, grab dinner at one of the small Caribbean eateries serving rice & beans with fresh-caught fish. No ATMs here—bring enough cash before you leave San José.

Head out on an early morning canoe or kayak excursion through the canals—ideally without a motor. The boat glides silently beneath the canopy: caimans sunning on the banks, spider monkeys and howlers in the branches, green and blue kingfishers perched in ambush. Wildlife is most active in the morning.

During nesting season (July-October), a guided nighttime walk on the beach lets you watch green sea turtles coming ashore to lay their eggs in the black sand. An accredited guide is mandatory. No lights or flash photography permitted.

Mid-afternoon arrival. First evening in the village: stroll the main street, dinner at one of the small Caribbean eateries with rice and beans and fresh fish. No ATM on-site—bring cash before departure.

Set out on an early-morning canoe or kayak excursion through the canals, preferably without a motor. The boat glides silently under the canopy: caimans on the banks, spider and howler monkeys in the trees, green and blue kingfishers in ambush. Mornings are when wildlife is most active.

During nesting season (July–October), a guided nighttime outing on the beach lets you observe green turtles laying their eggs on the black sand. An accredited guide is mandatory. No lights or flash allowed.

Arrive mid-afternoon. First evening in the village: stroll the main walkway, grab dinner at one of the small Caribbean eateries serving rice & beans with fresh-caught fish. No ATMs on-site—bring cash before you leave the mainland.

Early morning canoe or kayak excursion through the canals—motorless if possible. Your vessel glides silently beneath the canopy: caimans sunning on muddy banks, spider monkeys and howlers swinging through branches overhead, kingfishers perched in ambush. Wildlife activity peaks before 9am.

During nesting season (July-October), join a guided night walk to watch green sea turtles haul themselves onto the black sand to lay eggs. Accredited guide required—no lights or camera flashes permitted.

visits.text: Marino Ballena National Park, Costa Rica's first marine park (1989), takes its name from the humpback whales that migrate annually through its waters. Its main attraction is the Punta Uvita tombolo: at low tide, a sand spit emerges from the water and takes on the exact shape of a whale's tail when viewed from above. Walking on this sandbar, ocean on both sides, is one of the country's most beautiful experiences.

Nauyaca Falls, accessible from Dominical about 30 minutes from Uvita, ranks among Costa Rica's most spectacular waterfalls. The ~16 km round-trip hike can also be done on horseback. Swimming is permitted in the lower pool.

Marino Ballena National Park, Costa Rica's first marine park (1989), is famous for its Punta Uvita tombolo: at low tide, a tongue of sand emerges from the water taking the shape—from above—of a whale's tail. Walking this sandbar with ocean on both sides is one of the country's most striking experiences.

Nauyaca Waterfalls, accessible from Dominical (~30 min north of Uvita), rank among Costa Rica's most spectacular cascades. The hike (~16 km round-trip) can also be done on horseback. Swimming permitted in the lower pool.

Returning from Drake Bay reverses the boat journey to Sierpe in the morning, then drives north along the southern Pacific coast. Dominical, a surf village 30 min from Uvita, marks the gateway to the Costa Ballena. Playa Ventanas in Ojochal—a beach framed by cliffs pierced with natural arches—is among the region's most photogenic.

Free day in Uvita and surroundings. Hiking option: the San Gerardo de Dota area on the Inter-American Highway (~2h northeast of Uvita) is the other prime Resplendent Quetzal viewing site, set in a mountain valley landscape dramatically different from Monteverde.

activities.text: Boat excursion for humpback whale watching. Two migration seasons: December-April (Northern Hemisphere whales) and July-October (Southern Hemisphere whales). Spotted dolphins and bottlenose dolphins are present year-round. Manta rays are also regularly spotted.
Low-tide snorkeling near the tombolo. The coral reefs around Isla Ballena (2 km from the tombolo) harbor whitetip reef sharks, spiny lobsters, and scorpionfish.
Boat excursion for humpback whale watching (two seasons: December-April and July-October). Spotted dolphins and manta rays present year-round.

Transition and relaxation day along the coast. Beach at Dominical (surfing) or Playa Hermosa (calm swimming), lunch at one of Ojochal's small restaurants renowned for international cuisine, return to Uvita for the night.
Return to Marino Ballena Park for sunset on the whale tail. Entry is free in the evening after official park closure. Stunning Pacific views.
Snorkeling at low tide around the tombolo or kayak excursion through the Sierpe River mangroves depending on mood.
